title: Consent prompt vs cookie banner vs preference center vs notification preferences vs permission sharing vs human approval gate
summary: Use consent prompt when a user must make an active opt-in to a specific optional data use or participation purpose; use cookie banner for device storage and tracking choices, preference center for later account-level settings, notification preferences for channel subscriptions, permission sharing for granting another person access, and human approval gate for approving a proposed action before execution.
decisionRules: Choose consent prompt when the decision is a clear affirmative opt-in and active opt-in for a named purpose such as marketing, research participation, AI training, partner sharing, personalized recommendations, health-data use, or survey follow-up.
Choose cookie banner when the decision controls cookies, pixels, local storage, analytics tags, advertising tags, or similar storage on the user's device before optional storage is used.

Choose preference center when the user is managing ongoing account preferences and account-level settings after onboarding, including multiple settings that do not all require a fresh explicit consent moment.

Choose notification preferences when the user is choosing message channels, topics, frequency, quiet hours, digest behavior, or subscription state rather than consenting to a separate data-processing purpose.
Choose permission sharing when a user grants another person, team, guest, or link access to an object and the product must show recipient, role, scope, expiry, and revoke behavior.
Choose human approval gate when an AI, automation, or workflow has prepared an action and execution must pause for approve, reject, edit, timeout, or handoff.

A consent prompt must identify the controller or requester, specific purpose, data involved, optionality, consequences of accepting or declining, duration or review point, withdrawal route, and evidence record before the user chooses.

A consent prompt must offer a deliberate accept action and a real decline path with comparable effort; silence, inactivity, preselected choices, continuing to browse, bundled terms, or hidden refusal do not prove consent.
Use granular consent choices when separate purposes are offered, and refresh consent when the purpose, data type, controller, sharing partner, or expected consequence changes materially.

Do not substitute a privacy policy link, legal acceptance checkbox, cookie banner, notification opt-in, account setting, or approval dialog for a consent prompt when the user must knowingly agree to a specific optional data use.
failureModes: The prompt says continue to agree and treats inactivity or navigation as consent.
The accept button is prominent while decline is hidden behind a second page, small link, or misleading color.
Marketing, research, personalization, and partner sharing are bundled into one vague consent choice.
The user can grant consent but cannot later find where to withdraw it.
Consent is requested after optional processing has already started, so the prompt records agreement too late.
The product records only accepted=true without purpose, version, requester, data type, timestamp, or withdrawal state.

sources.note: Supports freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous consent, clear affirmative action, genuine choice, granular purposes, clear language, and no pre-ticked boxes or inactivity.
Supports consent recording, management, withdrawal, refresh, and evidence requirements.
Supports granular consent, clear affirmative action, easy withdrawal, and no silence or pre-ticked boxes.
Supports contextual timing, understandable copy, decision reversal, informed choice, and dark-pattern avoidance for permission-like requests.
Supports tying sensitive access requests to user actions, explaining private-data access, and handling grant or deny outcomes.
